text: The surgeon begins the peritoneal flap dissection at the medial umbilical ligament using an energy device, carefully developing the flap while maintaining a pre-peritoneal approach to avoid entering the pre-transverse salus plane.

The surgeon performs a blunt dissection in the medial region, maintaining the intermediate fascia against the rectus muscle to minimize bleeding. The visual highlights the green-colored visceral compartment, contrasting with the potential parietal compartment anterior to the intermediate fascia.

The surgeon is currently in the parietal compartment, dissecting the intermediate fascia and moving towards the lateral dissection. The areolar tissue planes are visible, and the surgeon highlights the transition into the visceral compartment.

The surgeon incises the intermediate fascia to access the visceral compartment laterally, reflecting the fascia and areolar tissue towards the sidewall to protect nerves and vasculature. The peritoneum is isolated in the surgeon's hand, while fatty and areolar tissues are reflected upwards. A small indirect hernia sac is reduced easily, and the surgeon begins developing the critical view of the myopectineal orifice.
The surgeon incises the intermediate fascia and focuses on entering the parietal compartment over the bladder. The parietal compartment is highlighted in green.

The surgeon reflects the intermediate fascia down towards the bladder to protect it during the dissection. The retro pubic dissection continues as the surgeon identifies potential hernia sites, including a small preperitoneal fat femoral hernia, which is reduced. The dissection progresses to reveal the lacunar ligament.
The surgeon continues the lateral dissection, reflecting tissue towards the sidewall, and proceeds to identify the round ligament in preparation for transection.

The surgeon tidies up the final dissection, ensuring the mesh will have a suitable landing zone. The iliopubic tract is clearly identified, a cord lipoma is rolled out, and the dissection is prepared for mesh positioning.

The surgeon places a mid-weight polypropylene mesh in the surgical field, ensuring it provides great coverage. The mesh is fixated to the Cooper's ligament and a single stitch is placed through the lacunar ligament for additional fixation. The procedure concludes with a standard flap closure, reducing insufflation pressure to between six and eight millimeters of mercury, and closing the flap from lateral to medial.
